title: Dog Sensitivity to Touch Along Spine: Causes & Triage Guide
meta_desc: Learn about dog spinal sensitivity, why it happens, and when to seek veterinary care for back pain. Urgency level: Medium.
content_html:
Understanding Spinal Sensitivity in Dogs

Sensitivity to touch along the spine is a common indicator of pain, inflammation, or neurological distress in dogs. You may notice your dog flinching, tensing their muscles, dipping their back, or even snapping when the area is touched. This sensitivity can be caused by various factors including muscle strains, osteoarthritis, or more serious conditions like Intervertebral Disc Disease (IVDD).

Is it an Emergency?

The urgency level for spinal sensitivity is generally Medium. While it may not require an immediate emergency room visit if your dog is still walking normally, it does require a veterinary appointment within 24 to 48 hours to prevent the condition from worsening. However, if your dog is unable to walk, dragging their hind legs, or has lost control of their bowels, seek emergency care immediately.

How Photos and Videos Help Triage

When consulting with a triage professional or your veterinarian, providing a video of your dog’s movement can be invaluable. Capturing how they walk (their gait) and a video of the specific reaction they have when touched helps the expert identify the exact vertebrae involved and the severity of the neurological deficit. This visual data allows for a more accurate assessment of whether the issue is muscular or related to the spinal cord.

faqs.question: What is the most common cause of back pain in dogs?
faqs.answer: Intervertebral Disc Disease (IVDD) is a frequent cause, especially in breeds with long backs, but muscle spasms and arthritis are also very common.

faqs.question: Should I let my dog exercise if they have a sore back?
faqs.answer: No, you should implement strict crate rest or restricted movement, avoiding stairs and jumping, until a veterinarian has performed an evaluation.

faqs.question: Can I give my dog aspirin for their spinal pain?
faqs.answer: You should never administer human pain medication to a dog without veterinary approval, as many are toxic or interfere with safer medications the vet may want to prescribe.
